title: Milk on Carpet
timeEstimate: 20 minutes plus fan-dry; odor work is the long tail
stainChemistry: Same milk chemistry on carpet. The stain often looks like 'nothing' fresh — the problem is the ODOR that develops from souring bacteria if the milk soaked into the pad and wasn't fully cleared. Protein-before-heat ordering is non-negotiable. Synthetic face yarn tolerates enzymes + NSD; wool narrows the lane.
whyThisWorks: Cold NSD damp cloth pulls soluble protein and lactose. Enzyme pretreat digests milk protein before any bacteria can metabolize it. Weighted dry towel extraction keeps moisture out of the pad — the invisible failure mode on milk-carpet is NOT the visual stain but the smell that emerges 24h later from bacteria in the pad.
defaultAssumption: Milk-carpet is an odor-risk stain more than a visual one. Extract aggressively and address the pad, or the smell comes back.

spottingProtocol.technique: Press dry, lift as much liquid as possible
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press folded white towels hard on the spill. Lift, move to dry section, press again. Get as much milk OUT before chemistry touches it.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD in cold water, damp cloth, weighted dry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold NSD damp cloth. Never pour. Weighted dry towel extraction between passes.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter / enzyme pretreat in warm water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dampen cloth with enzyme solution, press on spot. Digests milk protein that would otherwise feed souring bacteria.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD damp cloth, heavy dry towel + weight
spottingProtocol.instruction: NSD-damp flush. Weighted dry towel 30 min.
Fan-dry thoroughly. Dust baking soda over the area and leave overnight. Vacuum off next day. Baking soda + airflow = dry pad = no returning smell.
Sniff next day. If faint smell remains, repeat cycle. If strong smell persists, pad is contaminated = pro call.

steps: 2. Cold NSD damp cloth + weighted extract.
5. Fan-dry + baking soda dry dust overnight.
6. Smell-check next day. Pad contamination = pro.
warnings: Milk on carpet is an ODOR-risk stain — extraction + dry beats chemistry
Never pour liquid — pad saturation = smell

homeSolutions.agent: Blot Fast, Hard, Before Anything Else
homeSolutions.instruction: Press a thick stack of white paper towels or a folded white cloth hard on the spill. Lift, dry section, press again. Get as much milk OUT of the carpet as you can BEFORE touching it with anything wet. The more you extract now, the less work everything else has to do.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water + Dawn on a DAMP Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Dampen a white cloth with cool water + a drop of Dawn. Press on the spot, lift, rotate. Never pour water on the carpet — pooled liquid sinks to the pad where bacteria breed.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat on a Damp Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Dissolve OxiClean Versatile or Biz in WARM water per the label. Dampen a cloth and press on the spot for 15–20 minutes. The enzyme digests the milk protein BEFORE bacteria can get to it and start producing smell.
Press a thick DRY towel on the spot with a heavy book or pan for 30 minutes. This is THE step that prevents the delayed smell — it pulls moisture up out of the backing before it soaks into the pad.

Dust baking soda generously over the damp spot. Point a fan at it. Leave overnight. Baking soda absorbs residual moisture + pre-emptively handles any bacterial odor byproducts. Vacuum off next morning.

Sniff the area in the morning. If there's any sour milk smell, the pad has some moisture still and bacteria are working. Repeat the enzyme damp-cloth + weighted dry towel + baking soda cycle. If the smell persists after a second cycle, the pad is contaminated beyond surface treatment — call a carpet cleaner with hot-water extraction.

steps: 1. Extract milk fast — blot press + dry towels.
2. Cold water + Dawn on DAMP cloth. Never pour.
4. Weighted dry towel 30 min — the odor-prevention step.
5. Baking soda dry dust + fan overnight.
6. Smell-check next day. Odor return = pad contamination = pro.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if odor returns after two full cycles. Pad contamination = professional carpet cleaner with hot-water extraction.
warnings: Extraction is half the protocol — pour = pad soak = delayed smell
Enzymes die above 50°C — warm water, not hot
Milk on carpet is often invisible but smells later — treat anyway

neverDo: Never pour liquid on carpet — pad saturation
Never skip extraction + baking soda — odor is the real enemy
Never ignore fresh spills because they look invisible
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / olefin / polyester: tolerates standard chemistry

materialWarnings: Odor is the hidden problem on milk-carpet

escalation.when: Odor returns after two full home cycles, or wool carpet.
escalation.whatToTell: "Milk on [nylon / wool / synthetic] carpet, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with blot-extract + enzyme damp cloth + weighted dry towel + baking soda + fan. [Odor returned / faded]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with hot-water extraction

contextQuestions: Carpet fiber — nylon / olefin / polyester / wool?
Whole milk, skim, or plant milk? (plant milks can have different sugars + proteins)
How much spilled — cup / glass / bottle?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Milk-carpet = odor risk > visual stain risk. Extract + enzyme + dry is the whole protocol. Baking soda dry dust is the pad-smell preventer.
